ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Jalfrezi. Mapped to the broader curry food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 term exists specifically for Veg Jalfrezi. Mapped to the vegetable dishes category (A0CKM) as the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Veg Jalfrezi or Jalfrezi curry class. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (bell peppers, onions, tomatoes, cauliflower, green beans, carrots)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Veg Jalfrezi is approximately 300 g, representing one main-course portion as served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable curry, Indian style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No specific entry for Jalfrezi exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN entry for mixed vegetable curry (Indian style), which shares the same core ingredients: mixed vegetables, tomato, onion, oil, and whole spices. Macros are consistent with the dish profile. Sodium is elevated to reflect restaurant-level preparation typical of Singapore North Indian restaurants. Values represent a per-100 g cooked basis.

gi_source.title: Mixed vegetable curry — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable-based curry dishes and individual low-GI vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Jalfrezi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the Sydney GI Database which cluster in the GI 35–55 range; (2) the dominant ingredients being non-starchy vegetables (bell peppers, onions, tomatoes, cauliflower) which individually have GI values of 15–35; (3) the tomato-based sauce contributing modest simple sugars but no starchy thickener. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 22.2) / 100 ≈ 10.0, where available carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 7.4 × 3 = 22.2 g.

hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Veg Jalfrezi provides approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from non-starchy vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of about 10 per 300 g serving, Veg Jalfrezi is a suitable curry choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and rich in antioxidant vegetables and spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Veg Jalfrezi is low in saturated fat (approximately 1.5 g per 300 g serving) and contains no cholesterol, with bell peppers and tomatoes providing vitamin C and lycopene; however, sodium content warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Jalfrezi is a North Indian and British-Indian vegetable curry characterised by stir-fried mixed vegetables — typically bell peppers, onions, tomatoes, cauliflower, green beans, and carrots — cooked in a spiced tomato-based sauce with green chillies, ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The dish originates from the Bengal and North India region and became widely popular in British-Indian restaurant cuisine. The cooking method involves high-heat stir-frying of vegetables to retain texture, followed by simmering in a tangy, mildly spicy tomato sauce, resulting in a dish with a vibrant colour, firm-tender vegetable texture, and a bold, aromatic flavour profile. In Singapore, Veg Jalfrezi is served at North Indian restaurants as a main-course vegetarian dish, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI dish (GI approximately 45) providing approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g), vitamin C (28 mg/100 g), and vitamin A (62 mcg RAE/100 g) from the vegetable content. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content in restaurant preparations, which can be elevated. It is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease due to its low glycemic load and low saturated fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Veg Jalfrezi?
What is the GI of Veg Jalfrezi?
Is Veg Jalfrezi high in sodium?
What is the difference between Jalfrezi and other Indian curries?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Veg Jalfrezi exists. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry, which shares the same core ingredients and preparation method. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable vegetable curry entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient profile and preparation method are well-matched to the reference entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry (Indian style) as no specific Jalfrezi entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium value (310 mg/100 g) is adjusted upward from the ICMR-NIN home-cooking reference to reflect typical restaurant-level salt use in Singapore North Indian establishments. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Veg Jalfrezi has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero for vegan foods. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no vitamin-D-fortified ingredients are used and the dish contains no animal-derived sources of vitamin D. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as the dish uses vegetable oil and no partially hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish is fully plant-based and contains no animal products. 5. selenium_mcg is -1.0 — the ICMR-NIN source used for adaptation does not report selenium for this food category; this nutrient is not measured in the reference entry and cannot be estimated without a supporting approved source. The -1 value is used per the numeric hygiene convention for nutrients not reported by the source. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are mapped to broader class ancestors; no species-level ontology term for Jalfrezi exists in either system as of the accessed date. 7. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant portion sizes in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary between 250–350 g.
